title: How to Get an A* in A-Level Mathematics
author: Tklesson Team
content:
A-Level Mathematics is one of the most widely taken and most demanding A-Level subjects. An A* grade is required or strongly preferred for competitive university courses in engineering, economics, computer science, and the physical sciences. The subject is not impossibly difficult, but it rewards a specific approach to study. Students who earn A* grades are not necessarily the most naturally talented mathematicians. They are the ones who understand how the exam works and prepare strategically. Here is how to join them.

Understand How the A* Is Calculated

The A* grade in A-Level Mathematics is not simply a matter of scoring above ninety percent overall. The precise calculation varies by exam board, but the general principle is consistent. You must achieve an A grade overall, typically around eighty percent of the total uniform marks across all papers, and you must also achieve a high average, typically around ninety percent, across the A2 units, which are the papers taken in the second year of the course. This means your performance in Year 13 matters disproportionately. The A2 papers are where the A* is won or lost.

This has strategic implications. You cannot coast through Year 12, scrape an A overall, and then expect to earn the A* with a strong Year 13 alone. The overall A is the threshold. If you do not clear it, the A* is out of reach regardless of your A2 performance. But equally, a very strong Year 12 that gives you a comfortable overall A will not earn you the A* if your A2 papers fall short of the higher threshold. Both years matter, but the A2 year matters more for the top grade. Allocate your effort accordingly.

The Core Topics and Their Weightings

A-Level Mathematics covers pure mathematics, mechanics, and statistics. Pure mathematics, including algebra, functions, trigonometry, calculus, sequences and series, and vectors, typically accounts for roughly two-thirds of the total marks. Mechanics, including kinematics, forces, moments, and Newton's laws, accounts for roughly one-sixth. Statistics, including probability, distributions, hypothesis testing, and data analysis, accounts for the remaining one-sixth.

Your study time should reflect these weightings. Pure mathematics is where the bulk of the marks are. It is also where the most demanding questions appear. The A2 pure topics, particularly calculus, trigonometric identities, parametric equations, and differential equations, are heavily tested and often appear in the high-mark questions that differentiate A* candidates from A candidates. A student who masters pure mathematics and performs competently in the applied papers will score higher than a student who is balanced across all three areas but not outstanding in any.

Mechanics and statistics should not be neglected, but they should not dominate your study time. The applied papers are more predictable than the pure papers. The question types in mechanics and statistics repeat with relatively minor variations from year to year. A student who has worked through several years of past applied papers will have seen most of what the exam can present. The pure paper is less predictable. It requires genuine problem-solving ability in addition to content knowledge.

How to Study Pure Mathematics Effectively

Do not read the textbook and assume you understand. Mathematics is not learned by reading. It is learned by doing. After studying a topic, attempt every exercise question. Start with the basic questions to build confidence and check understanding. Move to the intermediate questions to develop fluency. Attempt the challenging questions at the end of each exercise, even if you struggle. The struggle is where learning happens. A student who only does the questions they can already solve is not improving.

When you get a question wrong, do not just read the solution and move on. Diagnose the error. Did you use the wrong formula? Did you make an algebraic mistake? Did you misunderstand what the question was asking? Did you not know how to start? The diagnosis determines the remedy. A formula error requires review of that topic. An algebraic error requires more careful working. A misunderstanding requires rereading the question and identifying key words. A failure to start requires studying similar worked examples to learn the approach.

Practice with past papers under timed conditions. The time pressure in A-Level Mathematics is real. The pure papers in particular require you to work efficiently. Start using past papers early in Year 13, not just in the final weeks before the exam. Each past paper session reveals something about your preparation. You might discover that you run out of time on the later questions. You might discover that you consistently make errors on a particular topic. You might discover that your working is too disorganized for the examiner to follow. Treat each past paper as a diagnostic tool.

Mastering the Applied Papers

For mechanics, focus on drawing clear diagrams. A well-drawn force diagram often reveals the solution before you write a single equation. Label all forces. Indicate directions. Mark known angles and lengths. The diagram organizes the information and reduces the cognitive load of solving the problem.

For statistics, focus on understanding what each test and distribution is actually measuring. Students who memorize procedures without understanding their purpose often apply the wrong test to a given problem. Know when to use a binomial distribution versus a normal distribution. Know the difference between a one-tailed and two-tailed hypothesis test. Know how to interpret a p-value. The conceptual understanding guides the procedural application.

The Bottom Line

An A* in A-Level Mathematics requires strong performance across both years, with particular emphasis on the A2 pure topics. Allocate roughly two-thirds of your study time to pure mathematics. Study by doing, not by reading. Diagnose every error from past papers and target your weaknesses. For mechanics, draw clear diagrams. For statistics, understand the conceptual purpose of each procedure. The students who earn A* grades are not necessarily the most naturally gifted. They are the ones who prepare strategically and learn from every mistake.
